messages.pickedPlainContent:
Hi Yongwen,

As of our most recent check-in, you’re on track to graduate from the Nest.

To stay on track, here are the areas to focus on:

   -

   Don’t just follow task instructions, understand the “why.” Make sure
   you’re grounding your work in the goal and constraints of the account
   before evaluating performance (e.g. understanding the objective before
   assessing FGX campaign performance).
   -

   Question numbers that look off. Sanity check and dig deeper when
   something doesn’t seem right (e.g. FGX Search campaign evaluations where
   very high iPCs like 5x or 15x were flagged without further validation).
   -

   Communication and confidence. You do speak well, but nerves can come
   through. Focus on slowing down and delivering your thoughts clearly and
   confidently.

Please let me know if anything is unclear or if you want to walk through
examples together!


Headed to Shoptalk

messages.snippet: Hi Ian, As of our most recent check-in, you're on track to graduate from the Nest. To stay on track, here are the areas to focus on: Question numbers that look off. Don't take outputs at face
messages.pickedPlainContent:
Hi Ian,

As of our most recent check-in, you’re on track to graduate from the Nest.

To stay on track, here are the areas to focus on:

   -

   Question numbers that look off. Don’t take outputs at face value, sanity
   check and dig in when something doesn’t make sense (e.g. FGX Meta campaign
   evaluations where you flagged a 5x iPC without digging deeper).
   -

   Don’t make recommendations based only on numbers without context. Always
   layer in strategy, goals, and your own judgment (e.g. recommending moving
   budget from Prospecting to Reengagement without considering acquisition
   priorities).
   -

   Go beyond the task and think about the next step. I want to see more
   ownership in understanding the end goal and proactively suggesting what to
   do next (e.g. FGX Meta non-DPA optimizations vs just executing what’s
   written).
   -

   Don’t just follow instructions, understand the “why.” Always ground your
   work in the broader account context before evaluating performance or making
   decisions.

Please let me know if anything is unclear or if you want to walk through
examples together!

messages.snippet: Hello WITHIN team! We're excited to share that WITHIN is moving our 401(k) Plan to Betterment at Work, a modern provider that offers an easy-to-use experience, personalized financial coaching, and
messages.pickedPlainContent:
Hello WITHIN team!


We’re excited to share that WITHIN is moving our 401(k) Plan to *Betterment
at Work*, a modern provider that offers an easy-to-use experience,
personalized financial coaching, and robust education resources.
What’s changing

   - Our current 401(k) provider is Accrue (formerly Guideline).
   - Accrue will process *final contributions through the 3/31 payroll*.
   - Starting with the *4/15 payroll*, your 401(k) contributions will be
   sent to *Betterment* instead of Accrue.

Your existing 401(k) balance at Accrue will be transferred to Betterment as
part of a standard conversion process; you don’t need to move that money
yourself.
What you’ll need to do

In early April, you’ll receive an email directly from Betterment with a *secure
link to claim your new 401(k) account*. When that arrives:



   1. *Open the Betterment email* and click the personalized claim link (it
   will be coming from support@betterment.com).
   2. *Follow the steps to set up your account*
   3. *Review your contribution rate *and make any changes you’d like
   before the first payroll that runs to Betterment (4/15).

Helpful resources

To help you get comfortable with the new platform, you can review these
resources any time:


   - *“How to claim your Betterment 401(k)” video* (what the invite email
   looks like and how to complete setup):
   https://www.betterment.com/employees/video/401k-claiming
   - *Employee 401(k) resources hub* (articles, FAQs, and short videos):
   https://www.betterment.com/employees/resources
   - *Video library* with short demos:
   https://www.betterment.com/employees/video

Live education session

We’ve also partnered with a *dedicated Betterment Client Success Manager*
who will host a tailored education session just for WITHIN on *Thursday,
April 9 from 3:30pm – 4:30pm*. In that session, they’ll walk through:


   - How to maximize use of your Betterment 401(k) account
   - Where to find ongoing tools, advice, and support
   - Any questions you may have during the live Q&A portion

We’ll send a separate calendar invite with join details.


If you have questions in the meantime, you can always reach out to me, and
once your account is live, Betterment’s support team will also be available
to help.

Best,
Danielle

--

messages.pickedPlainContent:
Hi Tirtha,

As of our most recent check-in, you’re on track to graduate from the Nest.

I want to start with what’s going well:

   -

   Strong communication. You consistently check in and confirm receipt of
   tasks, which is great (e.g. confirming receipt and following up on asks).

To stay on track, here are the areas to focus on:

   -

   Don’t just copy/paste AI outputs. Always review and make sure everything
   is accurate and makes sense before sharing (e.g. ensuring outputs are
   validated vs taken at face value). Remember: I'm not looking for the most
   polished, beautiful deliverable! I'm looknig for the best result in the
   simplest form.
   -

   Don’t just follow task instructions exactly. Make sure you understand
   the true end goal and then determine the best way to accomplish it (e.g.
   going beyond what’s written in the task to focus on impact).
   -

   Proactivity. Apply this mindset consistently across your work by
   identifying what else should be done beyond the immediate ask.
   -

   Sanity check numbers. Flag anything that looks off and dig into it (e.g.
   questioning FGX Meta campaign numbers that don’t align).

Please let me know if anything is unclear or if you want to walk through
examples together!

*   Don’t just follow task instructions exactly. Make sure you understand the true end goal and then determine the best way to accomplish it (e.g. going beyond what’s written in the task to focus on impact).
    
*   Proactivity. Apply this mindset consistently across your work by identifying what else should be done beyond the immediate ask.
    
*   Sanity check numbers. Flag anything that looks off and dig into it (e.g. questioning FGX Meta campaign numbers that don’t align).

Notes from “[INT] New AM Training Process Overview”

You are receiving this email because you are part of a group that was  
invited to the meeting.

Open meeting notes

The content was auto-generated on March 24, 2026, 4:35 PM EDT and may  
contain errors.


Summary
Account Manager training overhaul with increased homework and manager  
involvement was discussed, with external hires complicating program scaling  
and director time commitments.


AM Training Requires More Work
Participants agreed that the Account Manager training must include  
substantial homework utilizing AI and the platform, significantly  
increasing the time commitment from the current nearly nonexistent level. A  
decision was made to formalize a two-pronged approach, allowing trainees'  
direct managers to supplement the core AM training to identify knowledge  
gaps.

Timeline and Adherence Concerns
Concerns were raised about ensuring adherence to the training timelines,  
especially when competing priorities like sales pitches arise, which  
presents a churn risk for staff. The group suggested establishing clear  
expectations for the process, estimating it will take 1 year from the time  
someone becomes an S3.

Scaling and Prerequisites Defined
S3 candidates must pass the bimonthly review cycle and be in good standing  
before enrolling in the AM training process to ensure they are adapting  
well to the S3 role. The inclusion of external experienced hires in the  
training process significantly increases the program's scope, suggesting  
the need for automation and alternative structures like bi-weekly sessions.


Suggested next steps



[Areeb Mahamadi] Request Dates: Ask each individual director for a  
realistic target promotion date for current S3s.

[Areeb Mahamadi] Create Tracker: Develop a tracker system for S3s to ensure  
progress remains on schedule.

[The group] Submit Elimination Ideas: Drop ideas in the Slack channel for  
existing processes or tasks to eliminate.

[Areeb Mahamadi] Update Training Process: Factor in external hire  
requirements; update process based on Slack feedback. Redo documents to  
kick off new S3 training next week.


Meeting records Document Notes by Gemini


Is the Next Steps section in this email helpful?

The content was auto-generated on March 24, 2026, 4:35 PM EDT and may contain errors.

Summary

Account Manager training overhaul with increased homework and manager involvement was discussed, with external hires complicating program scaling and director time commitments.

**AM Training Requires More Work**  
Participants agreed that the Account Manager training must include substantial homework utilizing AI and the platform, significantly increasing the time commitment from the current nearly nonexistent level. A decision was made to formalize a two-pronged approach, allowing trainees’ direct managers to supplement the core AM training to identify knowledge gaps.

**Timeline and Adherence Concerns**  
Concerns were raised about ensuring adherence to the training timelines, especially when competing priorities like sales pitches arise, which presents a churn risk for staff. The group suggested establishing clear expectations for the process, estimating it will take 1 year from the time someone becomes an S3.

**Scaling and Prerequisites Defined**  
S3 candidates must pass the bimonthly review cycle and be in good standing before enrolling in the AM training process to ensure they are adapting well to the S3 role. The inclusion of external experienced hires in the training process significantly increases the program's scope, suggesting the need for automation and alternative structures like bi-weekly sessions.
